## Target Emotion
- First 10 seconds: grounded and in-control, with immediate clarity about today’s wake commitment.
- Wake state: focused urgency without panic; one clear action and no visual noise.
- After task completion: respected and steady; accountability is framed as continuity, not judgment.

## Visual Principles
- Layout density: medium-compact on home/list, low-density on active wake/completion to reduce decision load.
- Contrast model: neutral surfaces with strong text contrast; urgency is signaled by hierarchy and state labels, not aggressive color floods.
- Component shape language: iOS-native rounded rectangles and segmented groupings; avoid novelty containers that weaken scanning.
- Information hierarchy: one primary CTA per screen, one supporting status line, secondary actions visually demoted.
- Empty-state tone: instructional and calm ("here is the next step"), never guilt-coded.
- Native iOS alignment: SF Symbols, large-title/list conventions, system sheets, standard tab behavior, predictable back/close affordances.

## Colors And Tokens
- Semantic tokens:
  - `color.bg.primary`, `color.bg.secondary`, `color.surface.card`
  - `color.text.primary`, `color.text.secondary`, `color.text.muted`
  - `color.action.primary`, `color.action.primaryPressed`, `color.action.secondary`
  - `color.state.success`, `color.state.warning`, `color.state.destructive`, `color.state.disabled`
  - `color.state.focusRing`, `color.state.highContrastBorder`
- Light/dark behavior: hue-consistent tokens with luminance remapping per mode; do not swap semantic meaning between modes.
- Accent strategy: single calm accent (blue-green family) reserved for commitment actions and progress confirmations.
- Destructive states: red only for irreversible outcomes (delete task/account), not for missed wake messaging.
- Success states: green for completion confirmation and repair completion; never accompanied by confetti/gamified pressure.
- Disabled states: opacity + contrast-safe text fallback, not opacity alone.
- High-contrast fallback: explicit border and text token overrides for WCAG-safe separations on cards/chips.

## Typography
- Dynamic Type: all text styles scale using semantic styles; no fixed-size text in action-critical surfaces.
- Roles:
  - Title: `.largeTitle`/`.title2` for wake intent and active-task headline.
  - Body: `.body` for instructions and status.
  - Caption/meta: `.footnote` for timing/context labels.
- Numeric treatment: tabular numerals for timers, streak counts, and completion history values.
- Minimum readable sizes: body-equivalent minimum 17pt in wake-critical areas; footnote never below 13pt equivalent.

## Motion
- Transition principles: quick orientation transitions, minimal decorative movement, clear state-change feedback.
- Reduce Motion: replace scale/slide transitions with crossfade and static state swaps.
- Haptics: soft impact on start/complete; warning haptic only for explicit failure states.
- Timing ranges: 120-180ms tap feedback, 220-320ms state transitions, 0ms when Reduce Motion requires it.
- Wake-state restraint: no looping attention animations during active wake; only one-time state acknowledgment.

## Screen-By-Screen Implications
- Onboarding: 3-step progressive setup (intent, default schedule, optional strictness) with visible skip-safe path and reversible defaults.
- Home: top module is "Next Wake Commitment" with single primary action; metrics and history preview are secondary.
- Primary list: group tasks by readiness (`Tonight`, `Later`, `Paused`), with clear status chips and low-friction resume.
- Create/edit: sequence as `Intent -> Timing -> Safeguards -> Review`; avoid dumping advanced controls on first pane.
- Active task: full focus surface with one dominant CTA, current-state banner, and a plainly labeled fallback action.
- Completion: immediate confirmation, one suggested next step (log reflection or set next wake), plus optional repair guidance.
- History/streaks: show reliability trend + "why changed" annotations; include one-tap repair planning after misses.
- Profile/settings: separate Mission Controls, Account/Billing, and Help/Legal into distinct sections with different visual weight.
- Paywall/subscription: premium framed as reliability tools and deeper insights, surfaced contextually after value moments, never blocking urgent wake flows.

## Monetization Implications
- Premium promise: stronger accountability tooling (advanced safeguards, deeper history insights, adaptive planning).
- Must remain free: core wake flow, basic task creation/editing, completion logging, and non-coercive recovery actions.
- Dark-pattern avoidance: no countdown pressure, forced trial walls in wake flow, deceptive close buttons, or shaming upsell copy.

## Accessibility Risks
- VoiceOver: risk of ambiguous status chips and action labels; require explicit state announcements and action verbs.
- Dynamic Type: risk of clipped cards in home/list; enforce multiline-safe layouts and vertical growth.
- Contrast: muted neutral palettes can underperform; require token-level contrast audits in both modes.
- Reduced motion: animation-heavy progress transitions may confuse; provide clear non-motion state labels.
- Haptics-only feedback: never rely on haptics as sole completion/failure signal.
- Cognitive load: too many safeguards in active wake can create paralysis; progressive disclosure is required.
- One-handed use: top-heavy controls in large phones can reduce completion reliability; keep primary wake CTA thumb-reachable.

## Implementation Risks
- SwiftUI complexity: tokenized theming + stateful wake surfaces can fragment if not centralized in a design-token layer.
- Custom component risk: bespoke status chips/commitment cards may diverge from iOS behavior; keep wrappers around native primitives.
- Animation risk: state transitions across active/completion/history can create race conditions with async task updates.
- App Store risk: if paywall intrudes on core alarm/wake utility, review risk increases for deceptive gating.
- Localization risk: calm microcopy may expand significantly in some languages; design for multiline and variable button widths.
- Scope tradeoff: full reliability annotations in history may require backend/event model changes; phase rollout by surface.

## Source List
- `/home/daytona/workspace/.workflow/iphone-app-ux-studio/research/design-opportunity-synthesis.md` - consolidated constraints, top-screen opportunities, and anti-patterns.
- `/home/daytona/workspace/.workflow/iphone-app-ux-studio/research/competitor-flows.md` - competitor flow principles for sequencing and urgent-mode focus.
- `/home/daytona/workspace/.workflow/iphone-app-ux-studio/research/pageflows-research.md` - pattern evidence for progressive onboarding and single-action active states.
- `/home/daytona/workspace/.workflow/iphone-app-ux-studio/research/mobbin-mcp-research.md` - source-limited pattern guidance emphasizing adaptation-not-copy.
- `/home/daytona/workspace/.workflow/iphone-app-ux-studio/research/apple-hig-research.md` - platform-aligned accessibility/interruption constraints.
- `/home/daytona/workspace/.workflow/iphone-app-ux-studio/research/behavioral-ux-research.md` - calm vs urgent behavioral tone separation.
- `/home/daytona/workspace/.workflow/iphone-app-ux-studio/research/app-store-review-mining.md` - hypothesis-only pain framing and evidence caution.
- `/home/daytona/workspace/.workflow/iphone-app-ux-studio/preflight.json` - environment and evidence limitations affecting confidence.
- `/home/daytona/workspace/.workflow/iphone-app-ux-studio/research/reference-gap-analysis.json` - confirms earlier evidence gaps and why conservative inference is necessary.

## What To Adapt
- Progressive setup with reversible defaults and explicit next-step framing.
- Single-primary-action wake execution with persistent state clarity.
- Recovery-first accountability language after misses.
- Clear separation between mission-critical controls and account/legal surfaces.
- Reliability explanations (why streak changed) over pure streak gamification.

## What Not To Copy
- Exact competitor, Mobbin, or PageFlows visual compositions, spacing systems, copy, iconography, or motion signatures.
- Proprietary brand palettes, badges, reward metaphors, or onboarding scripts.
- Punitive/shame-driven wake recovery patterns and coercive monetization sequences.
- Any undocumented external claims presented as validated facts in this run.

- If encountered in source material: secret material omitted.

## Target Emotion
- First 10 seconds: immediate momentum, like “I can secure today’s streak in one action.”
- Wake state: focused urgency without panic; user should feel challenged but never punished.
- After task completion: earned progress and continuity, with a clear next micro-goal to return tomorrow.

## Visual Principles
- Layout density: medium on home/history (to show streak context), low on active wake (single dominant action).
- Contrast model: neutral surfaces with high-contrast semantic accents only on streak-critical states.
- Shape language: rounded-rect cards and circular progress marks; keep iOS-native control chrome.
- Information hierarchy: 1) today status, 2) next action, 3) streak context, 4) secondary controls.
- Empty-state tone: “start your streak loop” coaching, not guilt for missing history.
- iOS nativeness: SF Symbols, standard tab bar/nav patterns, native sheets, native toggles/segmented controls.

## Colors And Tokens
- Core semantic tokens:
  - `color.bg.primary`, `color.bg.secondary`, `color.surface.card`
  - `color.text.primary`, `color.text.secondary`, `color.text.inverse`
  - `color.streak.active`, `color.streak.frozen`, `color.streak.missed`
  - `color.state.success`, `color.state.warning`, `color.state.destructive`, `color.state.disabled`
  - `color.cta.primary`, `color.cta.primaryPressed`, `color.cta.secondary`
- Light/dark behavior: preserve hue identity for streak states across modes; adjust luminance only.
- Accent strategy: one streak accent family for progress moments; avoid multi-accent noise during wake flow.
- Destructive states: reserve red only for truly destructive account/task actions, not missed-day messaging.
- Success states: use green for completion confirmation, then transition to streak accent for continuity.
- Disabled states: desaturate + lower contrast but keep WCAG-readable labels.
- High-contrast fallback: replace subtle fills with 2px semantic outlines and stronger text contrast pairings.

## Typography
- Dynamic Type: all streak counters, CTA labels, and state banners scale with `UIFontMetrics`; no clipped numeric badges.
- Roles:
  - Title: wake status + streak headline (`Title2`/`Title3` equivalent)
  - Body: instructions and recovery text (`Body`)
  - Caption: metadata like “last 7 days” (`Caption1` minimum)
- Numeric treatment: tabular figures for countdowns/streak numbers to prevent width-jump flicker.
- Minimum readable sizes: 17pt body baseline; captions never below 12pt at default and must scale upward.

## Motion
- Transition principles: motion confirms state change (armed -> active -> completed), never decorative-only.
- Reduce Motion: replace scale/spring with opacity and color transitions; disable streak confetti.
- Haptics:
  - success haptic on completion
  - warning haptic on miss-risk reminders
  - no repeated haptic loops in wake-critical countdowns
- Timing ranges: 140-220ms for tap confirmations; 220-320ms for screen/state transitions.
- Wake-state restraint: one animated focal element max on active-task screen.

## Screen-By-Screen Implications
- Onboarding: frame streak loop in 3 steps (intent -> wake challenge style -> recovery promise), with skip-safe defaults.
- Home: top card is “Today’s streak mission”; secondary modules (history/profile shortcuts) move below fold.
- Primary list: tasks sorted by streak impact (today due, at risk, backlog) rather than creation date alone.
- Create/edit: include “streak difficulty” and “miss recovery mode” settings with plain-language preview.
- Active task: single dominant CTA, visible progress ring, explicit next-state label (“1 step left to lock streak”).
- Completion: immediate success confirmation + “protect tomorrow” action; no shame copy for near misses.
- History/streaks: show streak timeline with reason codes (completed/missed/recovered) and one-tap repair guidance.
- Profile/settings: split into `Account`, `Wake Preferences`, `Streak Rules`, `Support/Legal`; isolate destructive actions.
- Paywall/subscription: premium upsell on advanced analytics/long-range streak insights, never blocking wake completion.

## Monetization Implications
- Premium promise this supports: deeper streak intelligence (patterns, risk forecasting, advanced recovery planning).
- Remains free: core wake task execution, basic streak tracking, and standard recovery path.
- Avoid dark patterns:
  - no false urgency countdowns tied to purchase
  - no paywall interstitial during active wake flow
  - no withholding missed-day explanations behind premium

## Accessibility Risks
- VoiceOver: streak rings and badges risk unlabeled semantics; must announce state + consequence.
- Dynamic Type: dense history chips can truncate at larger sizes; require wrapping/reflow.
- Contrast: accent-on-tint streak chips can fail in dark mode without high-contrast fallback.
- Reduced motion: confetti/progress pulses can overwhelm; provide static alternatives.
- Haptics-only feedback: never rely on haptics alone for completion/miss signals.
- Cognitive load: gamification can over-clutter home; enforce strict priority stack.
- One-handed use: primary wake CTA must sit in lower-thumb reach zone on common iPhone sizes.

## Implementation Risks
- SwiftUI complexity: custom streak timeline + progress ring states can create view-state explosion.
- Custom component risk: over-custom badges/rings may diverge from design system and accessibility defaults.
- Animation risk: combined timers, haptics, and transitions can produce jank under low-power conditions.
- App Store risk: aggressive gamification language could be interpreted as manipulative if recovery is punitive.
- Localization risk: short English streak labels may overflow in longer locales; plan tokenized, testable variants.
- Scope tradeoffs: prioritize wake-state clarity and history reason-codes before advanced reward cosmetics.

## Target Emotion
- First 10 seconds: immediate urgency with control, like a safety checklist before takeoff.
- Wake state: high accountability, minimal ambiguity, no decorative distraction.
- After task completion: decompression and relief, then clear next-step confidence.

## Visual Principles
- Layout density: compact in wake-critical surfaces, standard spacing elsewhere.
- Contrast model: high-contrast foreground for active wake surfaces, neutral system surfaces for setup/history.
- Shape language: squared cards (10-12pt radius), strong edge definition, prominent primary action container.
- Information hierarchy: one dominant CTA, one state banner, one consequence line; everything else demoted.
- Empty-state tone: operational and direct ("No wake task armed" + immediate next action), never playful.
- Native iOS fit: SF Symbols, iOS sheet/navigation patterns, standard tab bar and large-title collapse behavior.

## Colors And Tokens
- Semantic tokens:
  - `wake.bg.primary`
  - `wake.bg.urgent`
  - `wake.fg.primary`
  - `wake.fg.muted`
  - `wake.action.primary`
  - `wake.action.destructive`
  - `wake.state.success`
  - `wake.state.warning`
  - `wake.state.disabled`
  - `wake.border.strong`
- Light mode: `wake.bg.primary` near-white; `wake.bg.urgent` warm off-red tint; text remains near-black.
- Dark mode: `wake.bg.primary` near-black; `wake.bg.urgent` deep red-brown tint; text near-white.
- Accent strategy: single alert accent for wake-critical controls, blue reserved for non-urgent navigation actions.
- Destructive states: red foreground + icon + explicit text label, never color-only signaling.
- Success states: muted green confirmation surfaces with short factual copy (no confetti treatment).
- Disabled states: lowered contrast plus explicit reason text under the control.
- High-contrast fallback: swap to system semantic colors (`.label`, `.systemBackground`, `.systemRed`) and add 1px borders on key controls.

## Typography
- Dynamic Type: all mission-critical text scales through AX sizes; CTA containers grow vertically instead of truncating.
- Roles:
  - Title: wake status and phase label.
  - Body: instructions and consequence text.
  - Caption: timing detail and audit metadata.
- Numeric treatment: tabular figures for countdowns, streak counts, and completion timestamps.
- Minimum readable sizes: 17pt body baseline, 15pt secondary metadata, never below 13pt for tappable-context labels.

## Motion
- Transition principles: quick, directional, state-explaining transitions (arm -> active -> complete), not ornamental movement.
- Reduce Motion: replace transforms with opacity swaps and static state banners.
- Haptics: single medium impact on wake start, success notification on completion, warning notification on miss.
- Timing ranges: 120-180ms micro state change, 220-320ms screen transition; avoid chained animations.
- Wake-state restraint: suppress non-essential animation while task is active to reduce cognitive load.

## Screen-By-Screen Implications
- Onboarding: 3-step max; step 1 defines wake intent, step 2 sets minimum viable schedule, step 3 confirms. Skip-safe path allowed with default schedule and later edit.
- Home: top module is "Next Wake Mission" with hard state label (`Armed`, `Due`, `Missed`, `Recovered`) and one primary action.
- Primary list: tasks sorted by urgency first, then recency; each row shows state token and next trigger time.
- Create/edit: progressive disclosure. Required fields first; advanced constraints hidden under "Strict Options".
- Active task: full focus surface with one primary action, one fallback action, and persistent consequence/status line.
- Completion: immediate factual summary (time, streak effect, next wake), then recovery options if partially failed.
- History/streaks: emphasize reliability trend and miss-repair actions over badge collection.
- Profile/settings: split sections into `Wake Operations`, `Preferences`, `Account & Legal`; dangerous actions isolated at bottom.
- Paywall/subscription: shown after value proof moments (post-completion/history insight), never interrupting an active wake flow.

## Monetization Implications
- Premium promise: higher reliability tooling (advanced schedule rules, deeper reliability analytics, smart recovery suggestions).
- Remain free: core wake/task loop (create, arm, complete, basic history, basic reminders).
- Dark-pattern avoidance: no countdown pressure on paywall, no fake scarcity, no blocking dismissal during wake-critical paths.

## Accessibility Risks
- VoiceOver: dense wake UI can become verbose; custom rotor labels are needed for state, timer, and primary action.
- Dynamic Type: compact urgent layout may clip metadata if component heights are fixed.
- Contrast: urgent tints can reduce legibility in dark mode if red overlays are too saturated.
- Reduced motion: state clarity may collapse if motion is removed without explicit textual transitions.
- Haptics-only feedback: misses users with haptics disabled; all haptic events need mirrored visual/text feedback.
- Cognitive load: hard-accountability tone can overwhelm if multiple warnings stack in active state.
- One-handed use: top-heavy controls conflict with reachability; primary wake action should anchor in lower thumb zone.

## Implementation Risks
- SwiftUI complexity: custom token system + multi-state surfaces can fragment modifiers and increase regressions.
- Custom component risk: reusable "Wake State Banner" and "Urgent Action Card" may require careful environment-driven theming.
- Animation risk: state machine bugs likely if wake transitions are modeled ad hoc instead of explicit enum-driven flow.
- App Store risk: over-aggressive urgency language or alarm-like coercion could be flagged as manipulative UX.
- Localization risk: compact hard-tone copy may expand significantly in non-English locales and break hierarchy.
- Scope tradeoffs: if time-constrained, prioritize active-task clarity and history explanations before visual polish.

## Current App Weaknesses
- Onboarding and auth are tightly sequenced before reaching mission value, as documented in intake flow evidence, which can increase first-run drop-off without a clearly cancellable or paused path.
- Tab intent is under-labeled for mission flow clarity; existing intake records Home/Runs/Profile structure but does not show visible evidence that users can infer urgency state from Home.
- Wake and completion flow states are present, but state transition confidence appears uneven across loading/empty/error conditions, creating ambiguity in urgency moments.
- Profile mixes operational actions (help/legal/account controls) with mission-support actions, weakening the separation between “wake execution” and “account management” goals.
- No validated PNG captures or screenshot manifest were produced in this environment due missing iOS simulator tooling (`xcrun` unavailable), so visual regressions for top screens are unsupported.
- Inference: accessibility coverage is present in some controls, but intake notes indicate incomplete coverage across profile/dashboard action states.

## Competitor Patterns
- Calm setup mode: competitor-flow references indicate progression is strongest when setup is staged and reversible; this aligns with reducing first-run friction and avoiding irreversible gates.
- Urgent wake mode: references emphasize a single primary action during active sessions; avoid overloading users with secondary decisions at the wake moment.
- Reward/accountability mode: competitor cues favor visible completion continuity and repair paths rather than punitive enforcement.
- Inference from competitor artifacts: tabbed structures are stable for orientation, but action density should prioritize mission visibility over ancillary settings at wake time.

## App Store Pain
- App Store review mining evidence remains indirect in this branch due evidence constraints in artifacts and network-limited sources; the file records themes as gap-sensitive rather than authoritative complaint sets.
- Inference: alarm/wake products commonly surface pain in setup friction, missed wake handling, and confusing state messaging; these are not confirmed user-review facts for this run and must be validated later.
- Actionable implication: treat review-derived hypotheses as provisional until full mining data is available with explicit evidence.

## Mobbin And Page Flows Patterns
- Mobbin MCP output in this run is source-limited and private-only: Mobbin could not provide full consumable UI artifacts for this branch handoff.
- Page Flows references are available as pattern material and consistently point to: progressive onboarding, single-action urgency, recovery-first completion, and explicit history reliability.
- Calm setup mode opportunity: collect intent and defaults first, then expose strictness and advanced constraints as optional next steps.
- Urgent wake mode opportunity: prioritize one urgent action with persistent next-step messaging and visible current state.
- Reward/accountability mode opportunity: present history/reliability context tied to repair actions rather than only streak decoration.
- Inference: these references support interaction principles; no proprietary UI or copy is replicated in this synthesis.

## Apple HIG Constraints
- Accessibility and discoverability must remain primary: controls and labels need clear semantic paths, larger touch targets, and predictable state transitions.
- Interruption-safe behavior is required in wake-sensitive moments: do not add motion or nested actions that delay comprehension in active state.
- Inference from existing source corpus: Apple HIG could not be directly loaded in this run, so constraints are framed as baseline platform norms plus the explicit accessibility evidence present in app files and baseline run output.

## Behavioral UX Constraints
- Calm setup mode: keep first-run and configuration screens low pressure, with optional expansion and non-committal defaults to reduce anxiety.
- Urgent wake mode: reduce choice overhead, maintain one dominant CTA, and communicate immediate consequence state so the user can proceed with minimal cognitive overhead.
- Reward/accountability mode: support completion recognition first, then accountability checkpoints with recovery language that restores confidence.
- Inference: emotional tone should be continuity-based (progress → recovery → retry) instead of abrupt switching between success/failure messages.

## Top Screens
1. Onboarding
- Weakness: first-run is linear and can feel mandatory.
- Principle: offer progressive reveal, explicit “what happens next,” and non-blocking path if a user wants to skip.

2. Home
- Weakness: mission intent is fragmented across cards and tabs.
- Principle: tighten the home dashboard around today’s wake intent before deeper navigation.

3. Create/Edit Task
- Weakness: setup work is split across states and can feel delayed relative to expected action.
- Principle: stage setup as confirm-intent -> configure -> review-start.

4. Active Wake Task
- Weakness: transition messaging is under-specified across implicit states.
- Principle: single primary action plus explicit state banner for next step.

5. Completion/Recovery
- Weakness: recovery paths are not visually documented from captures in this branch.
- Principle: provide quick recovery CTAs and short supportive summary that preserves motivation.

6. History & Streaks
- Weakness: accountability cues can become pure metrics without explanation.
- Principle: expose why streak changed and how to repair a miss.

7. Profile/Settings
- Weakness: account and mission-critical controls are not visually separated.
- Principle: isolate mission operations, support, and legal/account actions into distinct sections.

8. Paywall
- Weakness: paywall is gated in flow assumptions but screen evidence is unavailable in this run.
- Principle: keep payment and subscription calls clearly secondary and never block urgent wake completion.

## Visual Principles
- Use one dominant intent per screen in active and recovery moments.
- Preserve clear hierarchy for wake states: setup, execution, completion, repair.
- Keep motion purposeful and minimal, especially in urgent mode.
- Make empty/loading/error states visible with plain language and predictable controls.
- Keep tone continuity from onboarding through completion with supportive copy.

## Anti-Patterns
- Do not mirror proprietary layouts, copy, brand systems, badges, or exact spacing of external references.
- Do not use guilt/shame-based or coercive wording after missed wake missions.
- Do not hard-block users in onboarding or active wake with no skip/recovery pathway.
- Do not mix legal/account actions with mission-critical wake controls.
- Do not assume positive App Store or HIG signals without captured evidence.

## what_to_adapt
- Preserve WakeTask’s three-stage first-use sequence shape, but make onboarding skip-safe and explicit.
- Keep the tab shell if telemetry and architecture rely on it, while refining labels and card order to highlight wake readiness.
- Adapt WakeTask model continuity from setup → active task → completion using visible state transitions.
- Apply calm-mode first-run behavior: reversible defaults and explicit previews before strict mission options.
- Apply urgent-mode behavior: one primary wake control with clear “next state” language.
- Apply accountability mode behavior: streak/history logic that explains outcomes and offers quick repair steps.
- Expand accessibility labeling and state announcements in missing flows.

## what_not_to_copy
- Do not copy exact screen compositions from competitors, Mobbin, or Page Flows references.
- Do not copy any unclear or debug-like control path from current code artifacts.
- Do not copy hidden timers, silent transitions, or unlabeled interactive states during active wake.
- Do not copy generic placeholder content where the source lacks real evidence.
- Do not copy claims or interaction details that are not backed by the listed files.

## Direction Coverage
- Reviewed directions in `.workflow/iphone-app-ux-studio/design/directions/`:
- `calm_accountability_direction`
- `hard_wake_direction`
- `gamified_streak_direction`
- Cross-critique evaluates each role against each reviewed direction.

## Half-Asleep Usability Critic
- `calm_accountability_direction`: Strong recognition speed from single-primary-action framing and low-noise active state. Risk: medium-compact home/list density can still overload tired users if chip vocabulary is not aggressively simplified.
- `hard_wake_direction`: Best raw urgency clarity (`Armed/Due/Missed/Recovered`) and explicit consequence line helps error recovery. Risk: checklist tone plus compact wake surfaces can feel cognitively heavy when groggy; warning stacking could slow task start.
- `gamified_streak_direction`: Good momentum framing and clear “today mission” hierarchy. Risk: streak context and progress ring add interpretation overhead; tired users may parse “game state” before core action.

## Premium iOS Critic
- `calm_accountability_direction`: Most aligned with iOS restraint, semantic tokenization, standard nav/sheets, and non-gimmicky motion. Risk: custom status chips and commitment cards must stay wrapper-level over native controls.
- `hard_wake_direction`: Native patterns are mostly respected, but squared/strong-edge visual language may read less platform-organic if overdone. Risk: red-tinted urgent surfaces can look alarmist or non-systemic in dark mode.
- `gamified_streak_direction`: Keeps native chrome but introduces higher custom visual identity (rings, streak chips, reward framing). Risk: easiest path to “webby gamification layer on top of iOS” if typography and spacing discipline slips.

## Conversion Critic
- `calm_accountability_direction`: Credible premium story (reliability insights/safeguards) without coercion; good post-value upsell timing. Risk: may undersell emotional payoff versus more aspirational competitors.
- `hard_wake_direction`: Premium promise around serious reliability tools is clear and differentiated for high-intent users. Risk: hard tone can reduce affinity and trial conversion for users who want support rather than pressure.
- `gamified_streak_direction`: Strongest perceived value narrative for ongoing engagement and analytics upsell moments. Risk: if miss-recovery feels punitive, it can look manipulative and hurt trust.

## Accessibility Critic
- `calm_accountability_direction`: Good foundations (Dynamic Type intent, reduced-motion path, non-haptic-only policy). Risk: muted palette and dense status chips could fail contrast/VoiceOver semantics if not rigorously audited.
- `hard_wake_direction`: Explicit textual states are good for non-visual understanding. Risk: compact layouts under AX sizes and urgent tint contrast in dark mode are high-risk failure points.
- `gamified_streak_direction`: Explicitly acknowledges reduced motion and haptic alternatives. Risk: rings/badges/history chips are semantically fragile; must not depend on color/animation to communicate streak state.

## Anti-Clone Critic
- `calm_accountability_direction`: Safest from clone risk; pattern adaptation is generic and operational, not stylistically derivative. Main risk is converging on familiar productivity-card compositions.
- `hard_wake_direction`: Also relatively safe if tone/copy stays product-specific. Risk comes from overly recognizable “alarm challenge” visual framing if urgency modules mirror known competitors too closely.
- `gamified_streak_direction`: Highest clone exposure due to common streak metaphors, rings, and mission language seen across habit apps. Needs deliberate originality in copy system, icon semantics, and timeline rendering.

## Implementation Risk Critic
- `calm_accountability_direction`: Lowest implementation risk of the three; mostly disciplined IA/hierarchy and token work. Risks are manageable if history “why changed” annotations are phased.
- `hard_wake_direction`: Medium risk; requires robust state machine, strict tone consistency, and careful urgent-surface QA across themes/locales.
- `gamified_streak_direction`: Highest risk; custom timeline/rings plus multi-state gamification logic increase SwiftUI state complexity, animation/haptics coordination burden, and screenshot test matrix size.

## Scorecard Inputs
- `calm_accountability_direction`
- `differentiation`: Moderate; differentiated by calm accountability tone more than novel visuals.
- `native_ios_quality`: High; strongest restraint and HIG alignment potential.
- `wake_state_usability`: High; clear single-action model, low panic, good recovery framing.
- `conversion_potential`: Moderate-high; trust-positive, but less emotionally “exciting.”
- `accessibility`: High potential with chip semantics/contrast audits completed.
- `implementation_risk`: Low-medium; token and annotation scope are the main risks.
- `visual_distinctiveness`: Moderate; risks feeling safe/expected without stronger brand cues.

- `hard_wake_direction`
- `differentiation`: Moderate-high; explicit operational accountability stance is distinct.
- `native_ios_quality`: Medium-high; mostly native, but hard-edged urgency aesthetic can drift off-platform.
- `wake_state_usability`: High for decisive users; medium for exhausted users sensitive to cognitive pressure.
- `conversion_potential`: Moderate; compelling for serious users, potentially alienating for broader audience.
- `accessibility`: Medium; compact density + urgent color handling are meaningful hazards.
- `implementation_risk`: Medium; state architecture and tone QA are core risk drivers.
- `visual_distinctiveness`: Moderate-high; clearer personality than calm direction.

- `gamified_streak_direction`
- `differentiation`: High; strongest thematic identity via streak system.
- `native_ios_quality`: Medium; native scaffolding present, but custom gamified layer can degrade polish.
- `wake_state_usability`: Medium; good active-task focus but added gamified semantics increase tired-user parsing cost.
- `conversion_potential`: High upside; clear premium analytics narrative and retention hooks.
- `accessibility`: Medium; ring/chip semantics and color dependence need strong remediation.
- `implementation_risk`: High; custom components and state interactions significantly raise delivery risk.
- `visual_distinctiveness`: High; most visually ownable if executed with restraint.

## Consensus Recommendations
- Role-specific recommendation: Half-asleep usability and Premium iOS critics favor `calm_accountability_direction` as the safest core for wake-critical UX.
- Role-specific recommendation: Conversion critic favors borrowing selected momentum framing from `gamified_streak_direction` (post-completion/history surfaces only, not active wake).
- Role-specific recommendation: Implementation risk critic recommends avoiding full `gamified_streak_direction` scope in first release slice.
- Role-specific recommendation: Accessibility critic requires hard acceptance criteria before approving `hard_wake_direction` or `gamified_streak_direction` (AX text expansion, contrast, VoiceOver semantics on status artifacts).
- Integration suggestion for tournament scoring: treat `calm_accountability_direction` as baseline architecture, then A/B limited motivational overlays from `gamified_streak_direction` outside urgent wake flow.

## No Secret Output
- No credentials, tokens, private links, signed URLs, cookies, API keys, or user secrets are included.
- If encountered in source material: secret material omitted.

- `hard_wake_direction`: rejected because the urgency-forward tone and compact operational framing increase cognitive pressure in groggy states and raise accessibility risk in AX text and dark-mode contrast scenarios.
- `gamified_streak_direction`: rejected for phase 1 because custom rings/timeline semantics and gamified state logic add the highest implementation and accessibility risk, with elevated clone risk despite strong visual differentiation.

## Screen-Level Implications
- onboarding: 3-step progressive setup (`intent -> default schedule -> optional strictness`) with skip-safe path and reversible defaults.
- home: primary module is `Next Wake Commitment` with one dominant CTA; metrics and profile shortcuts are demoted.
- primary_list: grouped by readiness (`Tonight`, `Later`, `Paused`) with plain status chips and quick resume.
- create_edit: linear authoring (`Intent -> Timing -> Safeguards -> Review`) with advanced controls hidden by default.
- active_task: one dominant action, explicit state banner, and clearly labeled fallback action; suppress decorative motion.
- completion: immediate confirmation plus one next-best action (`set next wake` or `quick reflection`) and optional repair flow.
- history_streaks: show reliability trend with reason annotations (`completed`, `missed`, `recovered`) and one-tap repair planning.
- profile_settings: separate `Mission Controls`, `Preferences`, `Account/Billing`, and `Help/Legal` with clear visual hierarchy.
- paywall_subscription: position premium as reliability tools and deeper insights after value moments; never block active wake execution.

## Monetization Implications
- Keep free core loop: create/edit wake tasks, run active wake flow, complete, and view basic history.
- Premium scope: advanced safeguards, deeper reliability analytics, and adaptive planning insights.
- Conversion rules: contextual upsell only after demonstrated value; no fake scarcity, forced countdowns, or obstructive close behavior.

## Accessibility Commitments
- VoiceOver: every status chip, ring/state marker, and primary action uses explicit semantic labels and action verbs.
- Dynamic Type: wake-critical and list surfaces must reflow with multiline support and no clipped CTAs.
- Contrast: token-level audits in light/dark/high-contrast modes before release.
- Motion/Haptics: reduced-motion equivalents for all state transitions; never haptics-only signaling.
- Cognitive load: one-primary-action rule in urgent wake screens; secondary choices remain visually subordinate.

## Implementation Risks
- Design-token drift across wake/home/history surfaces if theming is not centralized.
- History reason-codes may require event-model updates and phased backend/UI rollout.
- Status-chip semantics can regress under localization and AX sizes without snapshot and UI-test coverage.
- Missing simulator tooling previously blocked baseline visual capture; hosted iOS validation remains required before UX sign-off.

The selected UX adapts abstract interaction patterns only and does not copy any external app assets, copywriting, branding, visual composition, screenshots, or proprietary interaction choreography.
